schools.summary: Ellicott and Clarke weigh 'again' and the 30,000/70,000 variant; Keil frames the muster as David making Zion the centre of the congregation's worship.
Henry reads the long neglect of the ark at Kirjath-jearim — 'perpetual visibility is no mark of the true church' — as now reviving under David.
Gill refers 'again' to the recent Philistine war, the same chosen men now turned to bring up the ark.

debates.question: How many were gathered, and what does 'again' refer to?

sides.position: thirty thousand (Hebrew, Targum, Syriac, Arabic), 'again' pointing to a former muster
a fresh levy of thirty thousand, though the LXX reads seventy thousand

schools.summary: Keil, Ellicott and Clarke identify Baale-Judah with Kirjath-jearim and probe the text ('from' vs 'to') and the doubled 'name' in the title.
Gill notes only that David went up with all the people that were with him.
Wesley: the great trouble was worth it because the ark was 'the choicest treasure they had,' before which Israel sought God.

debates.question: Does the text read 'from Baale' or 'to Baale'?

sides.position: 'from' is correct; Baale-Judah is a place-name (=Kirjath-jearim)
a textual error; read 'to Baale,' the outward journey being omitted

themes: the ark as symbol of God's presence
the Name of the Lord of hosts

schools.summary: Clarke and Ellicott: the new cart was well meant respect but a violation of Numbers 7:9; the law had 'passed out of mind' after two generations of neglect.
Gill: a great mistake — the ark was to be borne on the shoulders of Kohathite Levites; the Philistine precedent was no warrant, and he rejects Spinoza's textual conjecture.
Wesley: God winked at the Philistines' ignorance but not at Israel's, whose transports of joy made them inconsiderate of the law.

themes: the new cart as a well-meant error

schools.summary: Ellicott: the verse suffered scribal repetition, omitted in Chronicles and half-omitted in the LXX; Keil corrects 'instruments of fir wood' by the Chronicler's 'all their might and songs.'
Gill asks whether the thing 'brought out' was the cart or the coffer of the Philistines' golden offerings.
Clarke and Ellicott prefer the Chronicler's 'with all their might and songs' over 'instruments of fir wood,' a variant one Hebrew word wide but ancient enough to be combined by the LXX.
Gill: they played 'before the Lord' — that is, before the ark, the symbol of God's presence.
Wesley: public joy must always be 'as before the Lord,' terminating in him, or it is 'public madness.'

debates.question: Is the true reading 'instruments of fir wood' or 'with all their might and songs'?

sides.position: correct from Chronicles to 'with all their might' — the Hebrew text here makes no sense
Chronicles' reading is generally preferred, but the variation is ancient

schools.summary: Keil derives 'Nachon/Chidon' as the floor of the stroke/destruction, named from the event; Ellicott suspects it is no proper name and blames oxen turning aside for scattered grain.
Henry: a small deed but a real crime — a Levite touched what only priests might touch (Num. 4:15); 'familiarity... is apt to breed contempt.'
Gill reports the rabbinic tradition tying Chidon to Joshua's spear at Ai and locates the floor near Jerusalem.

themes: stumbling oxen and a steadying hand
the priests alone might touch the ark

schools.summary: Ellicott defends the severity as a necessary display against 'profane familiarity,' quoting von Gerlach, but holds the judgment temporal, telling nothing of Uzzah beyond the grave.
Wesley: God makes present examples against seemingly small sins for his holiness and mankind's caution — 'more of mercy than of justice,' the benefit reaching all ages.
Gill: the Lord's anger was made manifest in the smiting.

debates.question: What became of Uzzah beyond the grave?

sides.position: his eternal safety is not to be doubted; God's mercy reached his soul
the judgment was temporal and gives no indication of his state beyond the grave

themes: holiness not to be handled carelessly
a good motive cannot sanctify a forbidden act

schools.summary: Henry: David's anger matches God's own word — a lapse in the man after God's heart; 'when we lie under God's anger we must keep under our own.'
Gill: David's heart 'was ready to rise up against God and murmur' at so awful a stroke for so small a matter.
Ellicott: David 'was angry,' but not necessarily with God — vexed at an untoward interruption of his plans.

debates.question: Was David's displeasure a sinful anger against God, or grief at the interruption?

sides.position: a real fault — his heart rose against God, as if arraigning the divine justice
vexation and grief at the untoward breaking of his joyful plans, not anger at God

themes: a warning fixed to the ground

schools.summary: Keil: fear replaced excitement, so David turned the ark aside; Ellicott: the judgment produced a needed awe of God's majesty, at first excessive, soon moderated.
Henry: David's dread was partly slavish, keeping the ark 'at a distance,' but partly a right turning of the judgment upon himself — 'that others may hear and fear.'
Gill faults the fear, since David ought rather to have submitted, confessed his error, and been thankful for sparing mercy.

themes: How shall the ark come to me?

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott identify Obed-edom as a Levite of the family that guarded the ark, 'the Gittite' from Gath-rimmon, later a Tabernacle porter and musician.
Gill: David's setting the ark aside was prudence — waiting until he understood how to handle it without offence.
Wesley: Obed-edom, having seen Uzzah die, still opened his doors without fear — 'O the courage of an honest and faithful heart!'

themes: deadly to the presumptuous, welcome to the faithful

schools.summary: Wesley: 'the same hand that punished Uzzah's presumption rewarded Obed-edom's humble boldness' — piety is the best friend to prosperity, and the household shared the blessing.
Henry cites Josephus that Obed-edom's estate suddenly increased, and urges masters of families to keep up religion in their households.
Clarke: Obed-edom was blessed because he had the ark in his house — whoever consecrates his house to God's service receives God's blessing.

themes: the ark a blessing to a household
a savour of life to the welcoming

schools.summary: Henry: the blessing showed God reconciled and the ark no 'burdensome stone' — Christ, a stone of stumbling to the disobedient, is a precious corner-stone to believers (1 Pet. 2:6-8).
Ellicott clears David of jealousy — he resumes an unfinished purpose 'with gladness'; Keil stresses the ark is now carried, not driven; Clarke rejects the Vulgate/LXX additions.
Gill: the news of the blessing was brought to David by his courtiers.

themes: the ark no burden but a treasure

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil agree the ark is now borne by Levites per the law (1 Chron. 15), but read the six-paces sacrifice differently.
Henry: rightly using God's judgments means amending what was amiss; the opening sacrifice is atonement and thanks — 'begin with God' and speed in the enterprise.
Gill: David, having seen the former mistake, ordered the Levites to bear the ark on their shoulders with its staves.

debates.question: Were sacrifices offered once at the start, or every six paces along the way?

sides.position: along the whole way, at intervals of six paces
once at the beginning (and again at the end); repetition every six steps is unrecorded

themes: the corrected method — borne, not carted

schools.summary: Wesley: the dance was thankfulness after the manner of the times, the ephod owning David as the Lord's minister and servant, his royal robes laid by.
Ellicott and Keil honour the dance as holy enthusiasm and read the linen ephod as a religious, not strictly priestly, garment (worn also by Samuel).
Gill: David leaped 'as a lamb' for joy; dancing before the ark was unusual, which is why Michal despised it.

sides.position: a right and whole-hearted expression of holy joy before the Lord
doubtful — 'what connection dancing can have with devotion I cannot tell'; at most an involuntary elation

schools.summary: Keil marks Michal as 'the daughter of Saul,' manifesting her father's disposition — in Saul's day the ark was neglected and worship let fall (1 Chron. 13:3).
Henry: the shout and trumpet are testimony and invitation; free public worship under the smiles of the civil power is matter of rejoicing to a people.
Gill: elders, captains, and common people alike, as great a number now as at the first procession.
Ellicott: the scene contrasts the spirit of Saul's house, where the ark was neglected, with David's — Michal cannot grasp the enthusiasm that ranks a king among the common people before the Lord.
Wesley: Michal despised David as one of 'a base and mean spirit' who knew not the majesty becoming his place.
Gill notes the ark was now borne on the Levites' shoulders as the scene she scorned unfolded.

themes: piety looks foolish to the irreligious

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil distinguish David's special tent on Zion from Moses' tabernacle at Gibeon; the offerings consecrate the newly erected house of God.
Henry: David lodged the ark under curtains — not in a private house nor in a built temple, lest he supersede the greater one to come — and sanctified the joy with praise and prayer.
Wesley: the tabernacle was David's own tent, Moses' being still at Gibeon, for David meant to build the temple with all speed.

themes: David's tent versus the Gibeon tabernacle
a provisional glory awaiting the temple
joys sanctified with praise and prayer

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott compare David's blessing to Solomon's at the temple dedication; Ellicott distinguishes dedicatory burnt-offerings from eucharistic peace-offerings and denies this is the priestly benediction of Num. 6.
Henry: David prayed God to reward the people's honour to the ark — 'the less is blessed of the better' — showing them a king who loved them.
Wesley: David blessed as prophet and king, whose office it is to seek his people's welfare, chiefly by solemn prayer for them.

debates.question: In what capacity did David bless the people?

sides.position: as a priest — though blessing was a kingly prerogative too
as king and prophet, not exercising the prescribed priestly benediction of Numbers 6

themes: blessing the people in the Lord's name
a ruler who loves his people

schools.summary: Clarke, Ellicott and Keil parse the disputed portions — 'flesh' and 'wine' are supplied, the last term is likely 'a cluster of raisins,' the middle word uncertain.
Henry: a generous treat, a feast on the peace-offerings — 'when the heart is enlarged in cheerfulness the hand should be opened in liberality' (cf. Purim, Esth. 9:22).
Gill: David dealt a dole, dividing it among all the people.

sides.position: bread, a piece of flesh, and a flagon of wine (as the English reads)
the last item is rather 'a cluster of grapes or raisins,' the flesh-word uncertain

themes: the whole nation shares the joy

schools.summary: Henry: David returns to bless his household, yet meets Michal's scorn — her real grievance is his affection to the ark; 'the exercises of religion appear mean' to the irreligious.
Wesley reads 'uncovered' innocently — David stripped off his royal robes only to put on a Levitical ephod.

Clarke thinks David's violent gesticulations may have exposed some part of him, calls 'how glorious' strong irony, and notes David blessed his house as a patriarch-priest (Acts 2:29); Ellicott stresses Michal's cutting irony.

debates.question: Was Michal's charge of indecency true, or slander?

sides.position: slander — David observed decorum; her taunt puts 'false colours' on his zeal
perhaps some ground — violent gesticulations may have left him partly uncovered

themes: religion looks mean to the irreligious

schools.summary: Ellicott: David first gives the sufficient reason — it was 'before the Lord, in honouring whom no man can be really humbled' — then rebukes Michal by God's setting aside her proud father's house; Keil sees the reminder humbling the king's daughter.
Wesley: what was contemptible to Michal is honourable in David's eyes; God's transferring the honour from Saul's house obliges David to serve him with all his might.
Gill: David's act was for the honour and glory of God, so Michal ought not to have reproached him.

themes: the audience of worship is God

schools.summary: Wesley: the more we are vilified for well-doing the more resolute we should be — to 'be base' is to think nothing too mean to stoop to for God's honour.
Ellicott corrects the LXX's 'in thine eyes' to 'in mine own,' David ready to abase himself in his own eyes for God's glory; Clarke expands the retort against Saul's rejected pride.
Henry: 'the greatest of men is less than the least of the ordinances of Jesus Christ'; the truly pious are owned even by the consciences of those who speak ill of them (2 Cor. 5:11).

themes: nothing too mean for God's honour

schools.summary: Henry: Michal's scorn of David's devotion is repaid with the 'perpetual reproach of barrenness' — those who honour God he honours, those who despise his service are lightly esteemed.
Wesley: her barrenness was God's just punishment for her proud and petulant carriage toward David.
Ellicott ascribes it to human estrangement after the Phaltiel years, David resolving on no further intercourse; Clarke allows either cause and warns against Michal's own sin of rash judgment.

debates.question: Was Michal's childlessness a divine judgment or a natural estrangement?

sides.position: a direct judgment of God for scorning David's devotion
the outcome of alienation — David determined to have no further intercourse with her
either is possible — David's withdrawal or God's providence

themes: those who honour God he honours
